excerpts.excerpt:
Abruptly, a man in a gorilla suit leaps out from behind a
parked car and howls at them. Brendan falls down in fear.
THE GORILLA -- is cracking up, bent over with laughter.
BRENDAN
Jesus! Fuckin’ gorilla!
FRIEND
It’s just DeFord. He does that. Get
outta here, DeFord! You’re gonna
get shot some day.
DUSTIN DEFORD, 22, takes off the gorilla suit head, huge
smile on his face.
DEFORD
Shoulda seen your face!
FRIEND
Can’t believe you’re still doing
this shit. Grow up.
DEFORD
Gotta have fun somehow in this
town, man.
(MORE)
(puts on his mask)
And it is fun.

MAYOR
Natives. Hmm.
MARSH
It'll be like having a home town
ball team. Elite firefighters who
go anywhere, anytime. That’s great
publicity. Do this right, our Tier
One is a money-generating machine.
Instead of paying other Hotshots to
protect your town, the money goes
right back into the local economy.
The Mayor nods. Thinks about this. Finishes his bacon.
MAYOR
How much will it cost?
EXT. PRESCOTT - RUNNING TRAIL - DAY
GRANT MCKEE (21), trains for a half-marathon. McKee is a wiry
guy, his small frame all muscle, his thick wrestler’s neck
seemingly a part of his skull.
He runs alongside his cousin, ROBERT CALDWELL, only one year
older, huffing and puffing.

JUDGE (CONT’D)
Time served, and two years
probation. Next case.
INT. CREW 7 FIRE “STATION” - DAY
Marsh has a “waiting room” full of applicants. Guys dressed
in slacks, button down shirts. Some ties. Applicants have
fire fighting accreditation with them. Most are kids.
Then there’s Travis Carter. Jeans, baseball cap, T-shirt,
Oakleys. Kodiak dip in his mouth. There is an empty chair
dividing Travis from the other guys.
There’s the familiar scurrying sound in the walls.
APPLICANT
What the hell is that?
ANOTHER GUY
Ghosts?
They laugh, nervous. Then the sound again. Louder.
CARTER
Rats.
They all wonder where that came from. They look down the line
at this big guy at the end with his wraparound Oakleys. Huh?
CARTER (CONT’D)
In the walls. Rats. They bite.
THE OFFICE DOOR OPENS - and Eric Marsh calls out:
MARSH
Next!

MARSH
And of course the obvious -- fire.
It’s a hike of over 7,500 feet to the top.
MARSH (V.O.)
Ain’t working at McDonald’s drive
thru. Not working behind a cash
register or delivering mail, or
even something semi-dangerous, like
construction. This job? -- it’s not
for 99.99 percent of people.
MARSH - stands before the sun, his body in silhouette. Can’t
see his face, voice a low growl laced with North Carolinia:
MARSH
Takes a certain kind of insanity.
Dedication. Love for outdoors, and
grueling labor. Guys who have been
with me on squads before can tell
you what I’m about: doing. Not
seeming to do, or acting like
you’re doing it. Doing.
Young men, some shifting from foot to foot, some crouching,
others sitting in the dirt, watch him. A few have arms
crossed, looking tough, nonchalant. Others are wide-eyed.

DRIVING - Brendan looks at his reflection in the rear view
mirror. He can barely keep eye contact with himself.
INT. BRENDAN’S ROOM - DAY
He comes home and looks at his bong. Puts it under his bed,
out of sight, out of mind. Then peeks under the bed. Pulls
out the bong. About to fire up. Then puts it back.
INT./EXT. BRENDAN’S TRUCK - DAY
Brendan drives home from class -- sees the Crew 7 Fire
Station again. Slams on the brakes --
BRENDAN
Fuck it.
Yanks the wheel and pulls his into the station driveway.
EXT. CREW 7 FIRE STATION - SAME
Inside of the fence are piles of gear -- backpacks and tools,
all in neat rows. As Brendan walks up, he sees a YOUNG MAN
dump his gear over the fence and run off.
Steed walks out and starts collecting the gear. Shouts:
STEED
We got another dropout!
He doesn't see Brendan.
INT. GRANITE MOUNTAIN FIRE STATION - SAME
Brendan walks into the “bullpen,” just an open area with some
rudimentary furniture and an old TV. There is a strange
electronic WHINE in the air which bothers Brendan.

BRENDAN
I don’t know if I am.
David looks like he spends all day long in the gym. Not
winded at all. Gives Brendan a chummy salute and runs ahead.
MARSH (V.O.)
You are not only saving potential
lives, you are saving entire
landscapes. Forests; sometimes
whole neighborhoods and towns.
Think about that. Most jobs are,
“Do you want fries with that?” This
job is “Do you want fires with
that?” Looking Mother Nature in the
eyes and trying to channel her into
a place that doesn't kill people? --
that is an awesome responsibility.
CHRIS MACKENZIE PASSES BY - smirking.
MACKENZIE
Why don’t you give up, rookie?
Brendan glances at MacKenzie, then looks away.
ANOTHER GUY RUNS PAST. It’s DeFord, the guy who scares people
in the gorilla costume:
DEFORD
Brendan. Ain’t this a bitch?

WHITTED
I friggin’ won.
Exhausted, Whitted slogs to the center of a clearing at the
top. Throws the pack of his back. Beyond winded. Whitted
turns to see -- ERIC MARSH is already here, arms crossed.
WHITTED (CONT’D)
When the hell did you pass me?
MARSH
While ago.
Marsh is sweating, but not breathing hard. Waits for the
others. He’s got something in his hand -- a deck of cards.
Ashcraft arrives, then Thurston, Zup next to him.
THURSTON
Hey, Zup? You made it.
ZUPPINGER
(looking up)
Ha! I made it. I frickin’ made it!
Zup rushes over to the clearing. Flops down into the dirt,
spread-eagled on his back, eyes to the sky.
NEXT -- cousins McKee and Caldwell slog to a stop. They nod
to each other, congratulating one another without a word.
They are covered in sweat. McKee is bright red.
CALDWELL
Hey. What SPF did you use?

One Trainee waves his hands and drops out. He’s had it. He
walks off toward Whiskey Row.
MARSH
Where you going?
TRAINEE
To goddamn get a beer! Goddammit!
EXT. TOP OF GRANITE MOUNTAIN - DAY
Another grueling hike under punishing sun.
The 25 applicants make it to the top, led by, as usual Eric
Marsh. Second to arrive -- Steed.
MARSH (V.O.)
Two weeks left before fire season.
Push it. You will thank me later.
Marsh waits for the men to make it to the clearing.
BRENDAN -- sees what he’s got in his hands.
BRENDAN
Oh, man.
Cards. He’s shuffling the deck. Shuffling. Shuffling. What
might be taken as a slight grin is on his face.
Shuffle. Shuffle.
The sound of shuffling turns into CRACKLING.
The crackling of flames.

BRENDAN - is woozy. Feverish. Barely standing. Keeps going.
BRENDAN’S POV - foggy, tilting point of view. The world seems
to move around like he’s on the deck of a ship. He keeps
swamping, keeps moving brush.
MARSH -- gets a call on the radio. Nods and responds.
MARSH
(to the Crew)
Okay! That’s it.
Without fanfare, the work is done. The men pack up.
EXT. IN THE CREW 7 TRUCKS - DAY
Driving back from the fire, the crew is exhausted. Brendan
sits in the back. Face covered in soot. Sweating a fever.
STEED
That fire was a lay up, guys.
Brendan -- his eyes widen. A lay up? Holy shit.
ASHCRAFT
Felt like a tomahawk jam to me.
MARSH
That was good practice. But -- fire
season is officially on.
EXT. PRESCOTT - NIGHT
Brendan staggers to his house. Climbs the stairs, barely able
to get the key in the lock.

EXT. HILLSIDE - ANOTHER DAY
The Crew back-burns a dry and seemingly endless hillside.
MARSH (V.O.)
Take away the fuel, stop the fire.
A WIDOWMAKER -- falling from high above, nearly takes out
MCKEE, but Caldwell shoves his cousin out of the way.
The branch WHAMS! between them -- forty pounds of lumber and
sharp offshoots that could cause injury, if not death.
MCKEE
Whoa. Thanks, Cousin.
THE TORCHERS -- walk along with their flaming drip torches,
igniting piles of brush that Swampers have collected.
Brendan is one of them, dripping his flame on the fire line.
EXT. THE FIRE - NIGHT
The men work into the night. Brendan, red-eyed, feet dragging
in his boots, is surrounded by smoke and soot.
BRENDAN
Do we ever sleep?
Whitted, Steed, Carter, Ashcraft, Caldwell, MacKenzie, Zup,
McKee, Thurston, DeFord -- all work in unison, tools
seemingly constantly in motion. Steed grumbles:
STEED
Who needs sleep, rookie?
BRENDAN
Been working 24 hours, now.

AS THE HELICOPTER ROTORS DOWN -- MACKENZIE shouts:
MACKENZIE
Where’s your chin strap?
(Brendan looks at him)
No strap? No ride!
Brendan feels his helmet. No strap. Steed comes over.
STEED
What’s the problem?
BRENDAN
I -- I -- don’t know where --
STEED
Where’s your chin strap, rookie?
BRENDAN
It was just here! I swear!
STEED
Gonna make us miss this ride
because of your screw up?
BRENDAN
No. No, I --
STEED
(in his face)
You gonna make us all hike twenty
miles downhill because you don’t
have your CHIN STRAP?!

A SERIES OF SHOTS -- together, in sometimes hilarious
fashion, the two men take care of this tiny, crying infant,
feeding Michaela, changing diapers, baby sitting.
EXT. BACKYARD - DAY
An end of season barbecue is on, all the crew members, wives,
girlfriends, and kids present. Marsh hangs back in a corner.
BRENDAN
Sup. How you doing?
MARSH
Hey, man.
BRENDAN
Feelin’ good?
MARSH
Pretty good. Yeah.
BRENDAN
Goin’ snowboarding in the off
season?
MARSH
Nah. I don’t do stuff for pussies.
That’s the extent of their conversation for now.
ACROSS THE YARD - Claire and Amanda are talking. Claire
watches Caldwell do a ridiculous pop and lock dance for an
appreciative and slightly drunk crowd of Hotshots.
Steed joins in and starts an insane rap that is alcohol
fueled but hilarious to watch.

She nods. She and Marsh take off down the creekbed, hooves on
the rocks the only noise in the morning stillness.
EXT. BASE OF WOODCHUTE MOUNTAIN
Hours later, Amanda comes around a bend. Sees --
AMANDA
Eric! Got her!
-- a pure white horse, grazing by himself in a small meadow.
MARSH - behind her, on his own horse, moves toward the stray.
AMANDA (CONT’D)
Don’t wanna scare her. Let me try.
AMANDA - gets off her horse, lariat in her hand. Walks slowly
toward the lost horse, making soft sounds.
AMANDA (CONT’D)
That’s it. Gonna take you home...
She puts the rope over the willing horse’s neck. Cinches it,
not too tight. Firm. She smiles at Eric. Her smile fades.
She points. Marsh turns in his saddle to see --
-- A thin, pencil black line, leading skyward.
Smoke....

CALDWELL
Look how dirty it is.
Ash covers the edges, and the silver is scuffed. Claire takes
the band and rolls it between her fingers. She’s quiet.
CALDWELL (CONT’D)
What's wrong, Claire?
CLAIRE
Nothing. Nothing....
But she keeps looking at that ring, that metal band.
INT. MARSH HOME - STABLES - NIGHT
A HORSE is led into a barn. Amanda finishes her work. It's
dark, lit by an overhead hanging light. To the horse:
AMANDA
G'night.
MARSH
Hey.
She jumps, sees a silhouette standing there in the open barn
doorway, lit by the moonlight.
AMANDA
Eric -- scared the shit out of me.
MARSH
Sorry. I'm home.
(holds grocery bags up)
I'll cook if you're hungry.

INT. YARNELL INCIDENT COMMAND STATION - DAY
THE hotshot team SUPERVISORS go over the resources coming in:
engines, water tenders, aircraft. Marsh sees THE TEAM LEADER
who hiked past him, so long ago. The Leader nods at Marsh.
SUPERVISOR
Okay, here’s a good safety zone.
This little smudge -- that’s where
the vegetation has been cleared.
Marsh and the Captains study the terrain using Google Maps.
ON THE SATELLITE IMAGERY -- halfway between Yarnell and the
fire, Marsh sees a cleared area around Boulder Springs Ranch.
MARSH
That’s good. Bombproof.
EXT. FIRE ROAD - DAY
The Hotshots hike in single-file lines. Granite Mountain
takes the lead. Six other crews behind them.
STEED -- up front. Behind are four two-man saw teams and
squad boss Carter, followed by Brendan and the six other men
carrying Pulaskis and hand tools. Whitted and Caldwell next.
EXT. ON THE BASE OF THE HILL, YARNELL - MORNING
A little more than a mile in, the thin road veers left and
climbs 850 feet to the crest of the Weaver Mountains, where
the fire is burning. It is nearly 10 A.M.
MARSH
Donut! How hot is it?

EXT. ON THE HILLSIDES - DAY
Brendan gets a ride to a bluff in the valley that gives him
an incredible view of --
-- THE FIRE.
Brendan is left alone along the ridgeline. The crew are tiny
dots a mile away, cutting line. Worker ants.
EXT. HILLSIDE - DAY
Brendan sees another hill above him. A slight rise. He starts
hiking to the top, radio in hand. The loose earth and
chaparral make it a slog.
EXT. BRENDAN’S LOOKOUT RIDGE - DAY
From here, he can really see EVERYTHING, now. The whole
valley, the rising hills, the rolling landscape. And --
-- the incredible sight of the wildfire: endless carpet of
roiling flame on the valley floor. It’s so vast now that he
can't see the beginning or ending of it.
MOMENTS LATER -- Brendan finishes taking barometric pressure
readings and wind measurements.
Brendan then looks in the OTHER DIRECTION. Away from the
wildfire front. He sees --
-- THE TEAM, far below. Like yellow ants on a hillside, busy
cutting firebreaks. Marsh's voice comes over the tac radio.
MARSH (OVER RADIO)
What do you see, Donut?

MCKEE - has his phone. He starts texting.
This thing is making a push for Yarnell
and nothing will stop it.
INT. PRESCOTT HOUSE - DAY
Leah Fine sees a text come through from McKee as she hears
the news report of the fire on TV:
Wish I could come kidnap you
and take you away.
She gets a text a moment later:
Please do. I dare you.
OVER THIS, we HEAR:
INCIDENT COMMANDER (V.O.)
Fire hit the first trigger point. A
lot sooner than the estimate...
EXT. THE FIRE - FROM HIGH ABOVE - DAY
The fire hits the first trigger at about a quarter past four.
INCIDENT COMMANDER (V.O.)
It’s near the second trigger!...
THE FIRE - is a marauding, voracious beast. Fed by winds.
